subject: Recurring git-sync failures on chromabook — dirty working tree during autonomous sync

content.summary: Six escalation files in local-coordination (2026-03-13 to 2026-03-14) document a recurring pattern of git-sync failures on chromabook during autonomous cron-driven sync.

failure_types.description: git pull --rebase fails because the working tree contains unstaged changes from concurrent writes (another cron job or manual session)
Merge conflicts in transport/sessions/ directory when concurrent writes modify the same session files

content.root_cause_hypothesis: autonomous-sync.sh attempts git pull --rebase when the working tree holds unstaged changes from concurrent writes. The cron schedule overlaps with active agent sessions or other cron jobs that modify transport/ files. The fetch-reset directive (git-sync-convention turn 1) addresses transport/ conflicts but does not handle the broader dirty-tree condition.

related_context.note: Operations-agent already issued a fetch-reset directive for transport/ directories. That fix addresses merge conflicts in transport/ but does not prevent the dirty-tree rebase failures that account for 3 of 6 escalations.

proposed_remediation.description: Add git stash --include-untracked before pull, git stash pop after. Handles dirty tree transparently. Risk: stash pop conflicts require fallback logic.
Check git status --porcelain before sync. If non-empty, skip this sync cycle and log a warning. Next cron invocation retries. Simpler, no conflict risk, but delays sync by one cycle.

content.recommendation: Option 2 (skip-when-dirty) carries lower complexity and avoids stash-pop conflict handling. The sync delay (one cron cycle) presents minimal impact given the cron interval.

action_gate.gate_condition: Operations-agent reviews autonomous-sync.sh dirty-tree handling on chromabook

epistemic_flags: Root cause represents a hypothesis based on 6 escalation file patterns — not confirmed by direct autonomous-sync.sh log inspection on chromabook
The sixth escalation file (20260314T141407) appeared only in the second directory listing — may carry a different failure type
